<think>Reasoning process:

1. Clarify the evidence needed  
The question asks why the bottle of “Grace Browning” and extra vegetables are abruptly shown in a close-up that still centres on the white-bowl workstation. Relevant frames should therefore (a) include those two new items and (b) still keep the bowl and cutting board as the main action area.

2. Inspect high-score frames  
- 00:01:00 (score 5) – the bottle is introduced, purple nails hold it, and the cutting board now sports sliced bell peppers and onions: raw chicken still sits in the same white bowl.  
- 00:01:05 (score 5) – the bottle exits the shot but the camera lingers on the diced vegetables against the still-uncooked chicken in the bowl.  

No other score-5 frame appears. These two frames jointly show the moment at which the new ingredients are introduced near the bowl.

3. Compare evidence  
The vegetables appear seconds after the bottle is lifted, while everything else (the same white bowl, the same cutting board, identical chicken) remains untouched. At this point the dish has NOT been seasoned further—it is just sitting there—so this moment marks the transition from “raw chicken only” to “everything lined up and ready to be seasoned/cooked.”

4. Link evidence to the choices  
A (visual appeal): too weak—these ingredients are not yet arranged prettily in the bowl.  
B (nutritional improvement): speculative; the camera doesn’t make a health claim.  
C (shift in cooking technique): nothing shows new techniques or extra herbs.  
D (ongoing ingredient preparation): exactly what we see—new ingredients gathered at the workstation, poised for mixing.  
E (progression from raw to fully seasoned): premature; nothing has been seasoned yet except prior peppercorns.

5. Justify and act  
The presence of Grace Browning plus the vegetables simply frames the proto-ingredient spread that will next enter the bowl, i.e., ongoing leading-up preparation for the flavour mix. Therefore, choose D.</think>
<clip></clip>
<answer>D</answer>
